content.text: Chaining multiple LLM calls into a

— where the typed output of each stage feeds the next — is one of the most reliable patterns for building production AI systems. Rather than issuing one monolithic prompt that must accomplish everything, a prompt pipeline breaks complex tasks into discrete, auditable stages, each with its own prompt, optional tool calls, and a well-defined output schema that the orchestrator validates before advancing execution.

is an orchestrated graph of LLM calls (stages or nodes) in which control flow, data transformation, and error handling are managed by a surrounding program rather than by the model itself. Each node consumes a typed input — typically a Pydantic model or JSON schema — and produces a typed output that the orchestrator validates, potentially retries, and then routes to the next node or branch.
(a router node dispatches to different sub-chains based on a classification), or
(multiple worker nodes execute in parallel and their results are merged). The common thread is that the orchestrator — not the LLM — owns control flow, state, retry budgets, and observability.
Prompt pipelines sit one architectural level above raw prompt chaining. Where chaining simply connects prompts sequentially, a pipeline adds: an explicit
between nodes that prevent silent data corruption;
(latency, token count, cost, errors) for each node execution. These properties make pipelines suitable for production workloads where correctness and debuggability matter.

A single LLM call that must parse raw input, reason about it, look up external facts, apply business rules, and produce a formatted output will degrade in quality as task complexity grows. Each concern competes for the model's attention window and introduces error surfaces that cannot be independently tested or monitored. The monolithic prompt approach also makes it impossible to cache intermediate results or swap out individual components without rewriting the entire prompt.
Prompt pipelines address these problems directly. By assigning each responsibility to its own stage, each node can be independently
(swap one model without touching others), and
. A failed node produces a structured error at a known point in the graph rather than a subtly wrong final answer. This dramatically shortens the debugging cycle.

At scale, the performance argument becomes equally important. Parallel fan-out stages can execute concurrently, reducing end-to-end latency. Expensive stages can be given larger, slower models while cheaper stages use faster ones — all within the same pipeline. These properties explain why nearly every production AI application — from document processing to code review automation — is ultimately implemented as a pipeline, even when it does not explicitly use that framing.
— each stage has a single, testable responsibility; prompts are shorter and easier to tune.
— Pydantic/JSON Schema validation catches data shape errors before they propagate downstream.
— a failed stage can be retried in isolation without re-running the entire pipeline.
— independent stages run concurrently, reducing wall-clock latency for multi-faceted tasks.
— assign faster/cheaper models to routine stages and larger models to reasoning-heavy stages.
— deterministic intermediate results can be stored and reused across runs, cutting cost and latency.
— latency, token usage, and errors are tracked per node, enabling stage-level dashboards and SLO alerting.
— pipelines can be nested; a sub-pipeline becomes a single node in a higher-level orchestration graph.
The benefits of structured pipelines come with real costs that must be accounted for when deciding whether to adopt this pattern.
— multiple sequential API round-trips add network overhead. A two-stage pipeline always has at least twice the minimum latency of a single call, regardless of parallelism.
— every inter-stage contract is a schema that must be maintained as stages evolve. Schema drift between stages is a common source of silent bugs.
— managing state, retry budgets, timeouts, and error routing requires orchestration code that grows in proportion to pipeline depth. This code is not generated by the model.
— each stage incurs token costs for its prompt plus the context it receives. Pipelines that pass large intermediate outputs verbatim can cost significantly more than a single equivalent call.
— when a pipeline fails mid-run, reconstructing what each stage received and produced requires comprehensive logging. Without it, root-causing a failure across five stages is difficult.
— simple tasks that a single well-engineered prompt can handle reliably do not benefit from pipeline complexity. Adding stages creates maintenance burden without quality improvement.
The fundamental unit of a prompt pipeline is the

: a function that takes a typed input object, constructs a prompt (interpolating state from that input), calls an LLM (optionally with tools), parses the response into a typed output object, and returns that output. The orchestrator composes these nodes by wiring the output of one node to the input of the next.
The canonical approach is a single

— a dataclass or Pydantic model — that accumulates fields as execution progresses. Each node reads the fields it needs from this shared object and writes its outputs back to it. This avoids the temptation to pass large raw strings between nodes, which defeats schema validation.
A key design choice is whether each node receives the

of it. Passing the full object is simpler but couples nodes tightly; passing projections forces explicit contracts and simplifies testing. Both approaches are used in practice; the projection pattern scales better to large pipelines.

After each node runs, the orchestrator validates the output against the stage's output schema. If validation fails — or if the LLM returns an error, times out, or violates a business rule — the orchestrator increments a retry counter for that node. On retry, the orchestrator can inject a
: it appends the validation error to the next attempt's context so the model can self-correct. This pattern is called
and dramatically reduces the need for manual fallback handling.

per node (typically 2–3). If all retries are exhausted, the orchestrator routes to an error handler — which may return a safe default, escalate to a human, or abort the pipeline with a structured error containing the failing node's ID and last output.
Stages that do not depend on each other's outputs can execute concurrently using Python's
, a thread pool, or framework-level parallelism (LangGraph's parallel nodes, Prefect tasks, Temporal activities). The orchestrator tracks a

(often represented as a DAG) and dispatches stages as soon as their upstream inputs are available. Fan-out / fan-in is the canonical implementation: a splitter node breaks the input into N chunks, N worker nodes process them in parallel, and a merger node aggregates their outputs into a single output object.

The diagram below illustrates a production prompt pipeline with three sequential stages, an orchestrator layer, a parallel fan-out / fan-in sub-pipeline, and a validator node with a retry loop feeding back into an earlier stage.

attrs.alt: Prompt pipeline architecture showing sequential stages, orchestrator, parallel fan-out, and retry loop

content.text: (blue bordered boxes) receives a typed JSON input, issues a structured prompt, and writes a typed JSON output that the orchestrator validates. The
(dashed bar) holds shared state and dispatches stages according to the dependency graph. The
section (cyan arrows) shows stages that execute in parallel and merge their results. The
node (amber) intercepts outputs and triggers a retry loop (dashed amber path) back to an earlier stage when validation fails.
The task naturally decomposes into subtasks with different prompt requirements or models.
Intermediate results must be audited, cached, or reused across multiple downstream tasks.
Different parts of the task have different latency or cost profiles that justify model routing.
Error isolation is important: a failure in one stage should not silently corrupt the final output.
The system must operate at production quality with observable SLOs per stage.
Parallel subtasks (e.g., translating a document into five languages simultaneously) can reduce total latency.
Do not use a prompt pipeline when
A single well-crafted prompt can reliably accomplish the entire task — added stages mean added latency and cost.
The task is inherently sequential and cannot be decomposed without introducing unnecessary complexity.
Rapid prototyping is the goal — pipeline orchestration code is maintenance overhead that slows iteration.
The volume is low enough that latency and cost savings from caching and model routing are negligible.

The steps below walk through building a production-quality prompt pipeline in Python from scratch, without a framework, to make the mechanics concrete. Framework-based approaches (LangGraph, Haystack, Prefect) follow the same logical structure but add graph-level abstractions on top.
— create a Pydantic BaseModel with all fields the pipeline will accumulate. Mark optional fields that only some stages produce.
— each stage gets its own Input and Output model. These are projections of the shared state.
— a node is a function (or async coroutine) that builds a prompt, calls the LLM, and returns a typed output. Never embed retry logic inside the node itself.
— the orchestrator calls nodes in dependency order, validates outputs, handles retries, and accumulates results into the shared state.
— wrap each node call with timing, token counting, and error logging before writing to your observability backend.
— unit-test node functions with mocked LLM responses so the pipeline logic is validated independently of API availability.

# Minimal two-stage pipeline: extract entities, then classify them
from pydantic import BaseModel
from typing import Optional
import time
import json

# ── State ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
class PipelineState(BaseModel):
    raw_text: str
    entities: Optional[list[str]] = None
    categories: Optional[dict[str, str]] = None
    error: Optional[str] = None

# ── Stage output schemas ────────────────────────────────────────────────────
class ExtractOutput(BaseModel):
    entities: list[str]

class ClassifyOutput(BaseModel):
    categories: dict[str, str]   # entity -> category

# ── LLM stub (replace with actual API call) ────────────────────────────────
def call_llm(prompt: str, model: str = "gpt-4o-mini") -> str:
    """Replace with openai.chat.completions.create or anthropic.messages.create."""
    raise NotImplementedError("Plug in your LLM client here")

# ── Nodes ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
def extract_entities(state: PipelineState) -> ExtractOutput:
    prompt = f"""Extract all named entities (people, organisations, places)
from the following text. Return a JSON object: {{"entities": ["..."]}}

TEXT:
{state.raw_text}"""
    raw = call_llm(prompt)
    return ExtractOutput.model_validate_json(raw)

def classify_entities(state: PipelineState) -> ClassifyOutput:
    assert state.entities, "classify_entities requires entities to be set"
    entity_list = json.dumps(state.entities)
    prompt = f"""Classify each entity as one of: PERSON, ORG, LOCATION, OTHER.
Return JSON: {{"categories": {{"<entity>": "<category>"}}}}

ENTITIES: {entity_list}"""
    raw = call_llm(prompt)
    return ClassifyOutput.model_validate_json(raw)

# ── Orchestrator ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
def run_pipeline(text: str, max_retries: int = 2) -> PipelineState:
    state = PipelineState(raw_text=text)

    stages = [
        ("extract_entities", extract_entities,
         lambda s: True,                        # precondition
         lambda s, out: s.model_copy(update={"entities": out.entities})),
        ("classify_entities", classify_entities,
         lambda s: s.entities is not None,
         lambda s, out: s.model_copy(update={"categories": out.categories})),
    ]

    for name, fn, precond, apply in stages:
        if not precond(state):
            state = state.model_copy(update={"error": f"{name}: precondition failed"})
            break
        for attempt in range(max_retries + 1):
            try:
                t0 = time.monotonic()
                output = fn(state)
                elapsed = time.monotonic() - t0
                print(f"[{name}] OK in {elapsed:.2f}s (attempt {attempt+1})")
                state = apply(state, output)
                break
            except Exception as exc:
                print(f"[{name}] attempt {attempt+1} failed: {exc}")
                if attempt == max_retries:
                    state = state.model_copy(update={"error": f"{name}: {exc}"})
    return state
Example 1 — simple three-stage document summarisation pipeline
This introductory example pipelines a raw document through three stages: chunk detection, per-chunk summarisation (parallel), and final synthesis. The key technique is

import asyncio
from pydantic import BaseModel
from openai import AsyncOpenAI

client = AsyncOpenAI()

class ChunkSummary(BaseModel):
    chunk_index: int
    summary: str

class FinalSummary(BaseModel):
    summary: str
    word_count: int

async def summarise_chunk(chunk: str, index: int) -> ChunkSummary:
    resp = await client.beta.chat.completions.parse(
        model="gpt-4o-mini",
        messages=[
            {"role": "system", "content": "Summarise the following passage in 2-3 sentences."},
            {"role": "user", "content": chunk},
        ],
        response_format=ChunkSummary,
    )
    result = resp.choices[0].message.parsed
    result.chunk_index = index
    return result

async def synthesise(chunk_summaries: list[ChunkSummary]) -> FinalSummary:
    combined = "\n".join(f"[{cs.chunk_index}] {cs.summary}" for cs in chunk_summaries)
    resp = await client.beta.chat.completions.parse(
        model="gpt-4o",
        messages=[
            {"role": "system", "content": "Combine these chunk summaries into a coherent overall summary."},
            {"role": "user", "content": combined},
        ],
        response_format=FinalSummary,
    )
    return resp.choices[0].message.parsed

async def run_summarisation_pipeline(document: str, chunk_size: int = 800) -> FinalSummary:
    # Stage 1 — chunk
    words = document.split()
    chunks = [" ".join(words[i:i+chunk_size]) for i in range(0, len(words), chunk_size)]

    # Stage 2 — parallel summarisation
    chunk_summaries = await asyncio.gather(*[
        summarise_chunk(chunk, idx) for idx, chunk in enumerate(chunks)
    ])

    # Stage 3 — synthesis
    final = await synthesise(list(chunk_summaries))
    print(f"Summary ({final.word_count} words): {final.summary}")
    return final

# asyncio.run(run_summarisation_pipeline(open("my_doc.txt").read()))

This realistic example shows a four-stage pipeline used to auto-triage incoming support tickets: classify intent, extract structured metadata, generate a draft response, and run a quality check before returning. The quality-check stage triggers a retry if the draft fails.

import asyncio
import json
from enum import Enum
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field
from typing import Optional
from anthropic import Anthropic

client = Anthropic()

# ── Models ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
class Intent(str, Enum):
    BILLING = "billing"
    TECHNICAL = "technical"
    ACCOUNT = "account"
    OTHER = "other"

class TicketState(BaseModel):
    raw_ticket: str
    intent: Optional[Intent] = None
    priority: Optional[str] = None   # "low" | "medium" | "high" | "critical"
    summary: Optional[str] = None
    draft_response: Optional[str] = None
    quality_score: Optional[int] = None
    final_response: Optional[str] = None
    retries: int = 0

class ClassifyOutput(BaseModel):
    intent: Intent
    priority: str

class MetadataOutput(BaseModel):
    summary: str
    requires_escalation: bool

class DraftOutput(BaseModel):
    response: str

class QualityOutput(BaseModel):
    score: int = Field(ge=1, le=10)
    issues: list[str]
    approved: bool

# ── Nodes ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
def _call(system: str, user: str, model: str = "claude-3-5-haiku-20241022") -> str:
    msg = client.messages.create(
        model=model,
        max_tokens=1024,
        system=system,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": user}],
    )
    return msg.content[0].text

def classify(state: TicketState) -> ClassifyOutput:
    raw = _call(
        "Classify the support ticket. Return JSON: {intent, priority}.",
        f"TICKET:\n{state.raw_ticket}",
    )
    return ClassifyOutput.model_validate(json.loads(raw))

def extract_metadata(state: TicketState) -> MetadataOutput:
    raw = _call(
        "Summarise the ticket and flag if escalation is needed. Return JSON: {summary, requires_escalation}.",
        f"INTENT: {state.intent}\nTICKET:\n{state.raw_ticket}",
    )
    return MetadataOutput.model_validate(json.loads(raw))

def draft_response(state: TicketState) -> DraftOutput:
    context = f"Intent: {state.intent}, Priority: {state.priority}\nSummary: {state.summary}"
    raw = _call(
        "Draft a professional support response. Return JSON: {response}.",
        f"CONTEXT:\n{context}\nTICKET:\n{state.raw_ticket}",
        model="claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022",  # upgraded model for generation
    )
    return DraftOutput.model_validate(json.loads(raw))

def quality_check(state: TicketState) -> QualityOutput:
    raw = _call(
        "Score this support response 1-10 and list issues. Return JSON: {score, issues, approved}.",
        f"TICKET:\n{state.raw_ticket}\nRESPONSE:\n{state.draft_response}",
    )
    return QualityOutput.model_validate(json.loads(raw))

# ── Orchestrator ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
MAX_QUALITY_RETRIES = 2

def triage_pipeline(raw_ticket: str) -> TicketState:
    state = TicketState(raw_ticket=raw_ticket)

    out = classify(state)
    state = state.model_copy(update={"intent": out.intent, "priority": out.priority})

    out = extract_metadata(state)
    state = state.model_copy(update={"summary": out.summary})

    for attempt in range(MAX_QUALITY_RETRIES + 1):
        draft = draft_response(state)
        state = state.model_copy(update={"draft_response": draft.response})
        qc = quality_check(state)
        state = state.model_copy(update={"quality_score": qc.score})
        if qc.approved or attempt == MAX_QUALITY_RETRIES:
            state = state.model_copy(update={"final_response": draft.response, "retries": attempt})
            break
        print(f"Quality check failed (score={qc.score}), issues: {qc.issues}. Retrying draft...")

    return state

A legal document review platform processes thousands of contracts per month. Each contract goes through a seven-stage pipeline: (1) PDF extraction and segmentation, (2) clause classification (which model: a fine-tuned claude-3-haiku variant), (3) risk scoring per clause (claude-3-5-sonnet), (4) parallel cross-referencing against a clause database via tool calls, (5) summary generation, (6) a compliance check against jurisdiction-specific rules, and (7) a reviewer-facing report assembly stage.

The pipeline runs on Temporal (workflow orchestration) with each stage implemented as a Temporal Activity. Temporal handles retries, timeouts, and durable execution — if the service crashes mid-pipeline, execution resumes from the last completed activity checkpoint, not from the start. Stage 4 (cross-referencing) executes ten tool calls in parallel via
, reducing p95 latency for that stage from 12 s to 1.4 s.

Observability is implemented via OpenTelemetry spans emitted from each activity. A Grafana dashboard shows per-stage latency, token consumption, retry rates, and cost per contract. Stage 3 (risk scoring) accounts for 68% of total token cost, which drove the team's decision to cache its outputs for 30 days keyed on a SHA-256 hash of the clause text — reducing repeat contract cost by roughly 40%.

# Simplified Temporal activity skeleton for one pipeline stage
from temporalio import activity
from pydantic import BaseModel
import opentelemetry.trace as otel

tracer = otel.get_tracer("contract-pipeline")

class ClauseRiskInput(BaseModel):
    clause_text: str
    clause_type: str
    jurisdiction: str

class ClauseRiskOutput(BaseModel):
    risk_level: str   # "low" | "medium" | "high" | "critical"
    rationale: str
    flags: list[str]

@activity.defn
async def score_clause_risk(inp: ClauseRiskInput) -> ClauseRiskOutput:
    with tracer.start_as_current_span("score_clause_risk") as span:
        span.set_attribute("clause_type", inp.clause_type)
        span.set_attribute("jurisdiction", inp.jurisdiction)
        # ... call LLM, parse output, return typed result
        ...
Passing raw strings between stages instead of typed objects
When a stage returns a free-form string and the next stage parses it with a regex or

, a minor variation in the LLM's output format silently corrupts all downstream stages. The fix is to require structured output at every stage boundary using Pydantic models and validated JSON responses.

Wrapping the entire pipeline in a retry loop means that a transient failure in stage 5 of a 6-stage pipeline re-runs stages 1–4 unnecessarily — wasting tokens and time. Retries should be scoped to the individual failing node, not the top-level pipeline call.

Operators who add logging only after a production incident must reconstruct failures from incomplete data. Observability (per-node timing, token counts, input/output snapshots at DEBUG level) should be wired in from the first deployment. The marginal cost is low; the debugging value is high.

Passing the full accumulated state to every stage means each LLM call's context window grows with pipeline depth. A stage that only needs two fields from state should receive a projection of those two fields, not the entire state object. Otherwise, token costs and latency compound across stages.
— define all inter-stage schemas before writing prompt text. Schemas are the pipeline's API contract.
— if a node's docstring requires 'and', split it into two nodes.
— never advance state with an unvalidated node output. Fail fast at the boundary.
Separate prompt templates from orchestration code
— store prompts as versioned strings or files so they can be tuned without touching orchestration logic.
— prefix every log line with the node name and run ID so traces are grep-able across stages.
— any node whose output depends only on its input and a frozen model version is a caching candidate. Key on a hash of (node_id, model_version, input_hash).
— LLM calls can stall. A per-node timeout that returns a retryable error is safer than a global pipeline timeout that provides no diagnostic information.
— maintain a small set of fixed inputs with expected outputs (not exact strings, but structural assertions) to catch prompt regressions across releases.

For a linear pipeline with N stages, the minimum end-to-end latency is the sum of per-stage latencies. For a pipeline with M parallel branches, the critical path latency is the maximum branch latency plus sequential stages. Identifying the critical path is the first step in latency optimisation — parallelising stages that are not on the critical path yields no benefit.

Prompt length is a primary driver of per-stage latency because it determines time-to-first-token. Keeping each stage's context window as small as possible (projections rather than full state) reduces both latency and cost. For stages with deterministic inputs, semantic or exact caching can eliminate LLM latency entirely for repeat invocations.

Throughput scales horizontally: multiple pipeline runs can execute concurrently up to the LLM provider's rate limit. Rate-limit-aware clients (with token bucket or leaky bucket logic) are essential when running pipelines at high throughput to avoid 429 errors that trigger expensive retries.

Batching is effective when multiple pipeline runs share the same early-stage model and the model API supports batching (Anthropic Batch API, OpenAI Batch API). A batch of 100 classification stage calls can reduce per-call cost by 50% on some providers, at the cost of higher latency for each individual item.

Total cost per pipeline run is the sum of per-stage costs. Stage cost equals input tokens × input price + output tokens × output price. The dominant cost driver is usually the stage with the largest context window multiplied by the most expensive model. Audit token counts per stage before assigning models, and consider smaller models for classification or routing stages where accuracy requirements are lower.

LangGraph models a pipeline as a typed state graph. Nodes are Python functions annotated with their state inputs and outputs. Edges define the dependency graph, including conditional edges for routing. Built-in checkpointing and streaming are primary differentiators. LangGraph is well-suited for pipelines that need human-in-the-loop pauses and resume semantics.

from langgraph.graph import StateGraph, START, END
from typing import TypedDict, Optional

class State(TypedDict):
    text: str
    entities: Optional[list[str]]
    summary: Optional[str]

def extract(state: State) -> dict:
    # call LLM, return partial state update
    return {"entities": ["Alice", "Acme Corp"]}

def summarise(state: State) -> dict:
    return {"summary": f"Document mentions: {state['entities']}"}

builder = StateGraph(State)
builder.add_node("extract", extract)
builder.add_node("summarise", summarise)
builder.add_edge(START, "extract")
builder.add_edge("extract", "summarise")
builder.add_edge("summarise", END)

graph = builder.compile()
result = graph.invoke({"text": "Alice joined Acme Corp last month."})
print(result["summary"])

Haystack (deepset) is a component-based pipeline framework oriented toward document processing and RAG. Pipelines are assembled from typed components connected by named ports. Haystack's pipeline YAML serialisation enables version-controlled pipeline definitions and visual editors.

For pipelines with strict reliability requirements — durable execution across crashes, long-running tasks, complex retry policies — workflow orchestration engines like Prefect or Temporal are appropriate. Each pipeline stage becomes a task or activity with its own retry budget, timeout, and execution history. Temporal in particular provides event-sourced execution state, making it possible to resume a pipeline from any completed stage after a process restart.

library wraps the OpenAI, Anthropic, and other clients to enforce Pydantic-validated structured outputs from LLM calls. It handles retries for malformed JSON and supports streaming structured outputs. Using instructor inside pipeline nodes eliminates the need to write custom JSON parsing and validation code per stage.

import instructor
from anthropic import Anthropic
from pydantic import BaseModel

client = instructor.from_anthropic(Anthropic())

class SentimentOutput(BaseModel):
    sentiment: str   # "positive" | "neutral" | "negative"
    confidence: float
    key_phrases: list[str]

def analyse_sentiment(text: str) -> SentimentOutput:
    return client.messages.create(
        model="claude-3-5-haiku-20241022",
        max_tokens=256,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": f"Analyse sentiment of: {text}"}],
        response_model=SentimentOutput,
    )

In a pipeline, the output of one LLM call becomes part of the prompt for the next. If the first stage processes untrusted user input, an attacker can embed instructions in that input designed to manipulate later stages — a form of

. Mitigate this by sanitising user-controlled strings before interpolating them into downstream prompts, using structured output schemas that limit what the model can say, and treating every LLM output as untrusted input to the next stage's schema validator.

Pipeline state accumulates data across stages. If the state object is logged verbatim for debugging, sensitive fields (PII, credentials, legal text) are written to log storage that may have broader access than the pipeline's data store. Use field-level redaction on state objects before logging, and store raw LLM outputs (which may contain echoed user data) in access-controlled storage separate from operational logs.

In failure scenarios, a pipeline stage may execute more than once. Stages that write to external systems (databases, email APIs, payment processors) must be idempotent — running them twice must produce the same external effect as running them once. Implement idempotency by including a pipeline run ID and stage ID in every external write and checking for existing records before writing.
Under sustained load, a pipeline that retries failed LLM calls with fixed-interval retries can amplify load against an already-throttled API, triggering a retry storm. Use
for LLM client retries, and implement circuit breaker logic at the orchestrator level so that a sustained provider outage shuts down the pipeline gracefully rather than exhausting retry budgets.

Think of a prompt pipeline as an assembly line in a factory. Each workstation (stage) has a specific job, accepts parts in a known form (typed input), and passes finished sub-assemblies to the next station in a standard container (typed output). The factory floor manager (orchestrator) knows the order of stations, keeps a manifest of work in progress (shared state), can send a sub-assembly back for rework (retry), and tracks how long each station took (observability). No single workstation needs to understand the whole factory — it only needs to know its own inputs and outputs. This modularity is exactly what makes the factory (and the pipeline) easier to build, test, and improve incrementally.
A prompt pipeline is an orchestrator-managed DAG of LLM calls with typed schema contracts between every node — not merely a sequence of concatenated prompts.
Every inter-stage boundary must be a validated schema: untyped string passing is the single most common source of silent pipeline failures.
Retries belong at the node level, not the pipeline level; retrying the entire pipeline to recover from a single-stage transient error wastes tokens and increases latency.
Fan-out / fan-in parallelism reduces critical-path latency for independent subtasks; identify the critical path before parallelising.
Deterministic stages are caching candidates: key on (node_id, model_version, input_hash) to eliminate repeat LLM costs for identical inputs.
Observability must be built in from the first deployment: per-node latency, token counts, retry rates, and cost are the minimum instrumentation set for production pipelines.
Prompt injection can traverse stage boundaries — treat every LLM output as untrusted input and validate it against the downstream stage's schema before interpolating it into the next prompt.

LangGraph, Haystack, Temporal, and Prefect each encode the same pipeline pattern at different abstraction levels; choose the tool whose retry, checkpointing, and observability model matches your reliability requirements.
